children.name: Balaena
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the bowhead whale, the only great whale that lives its entire life in Arctic and subarctic waters, distinguished by its massive arched jaw and ability to break through sea ice.

children.name: Eubalaena
children.description: Comprises 3 species of right whales found in the North Atlantic, North Pacific, and Southern Ocean, recognised by their rotund bodies, lack of a dorsal fin, and distinctive white callosities on the head.

children.name: Caperea
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pygmy right whale, the smallest and most enigmatic baleen whale, found only in cool temperate waters of the Southern Hemisphere.

children.name: Eschrichtius
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the gray whale, notable for its 20,000 km annual migrations between Arctic feeding grounds and subtropical breeding lagoons along the Pacific coast of North America and Asia.

children.name: Balaenoptera
children.description: The largest rorqual genus with 8 species including the blue whale (the largest animal ever known), found in all world's oceans and united by their streamlined bodies and pleated throat pouches for lunge-feeding.

children.name: Megaptera
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the humpback whale, known worldwide for its complex songs, acrobatic breaching, and exceptionally long pectoral fins, inhabiting all oceans from the poles to the tropics.

children.name: Physeter
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the sperm whale, the largest toothed predator on Earth, distinguished by its enormous squarish head housing the spermaceti organ, and capable of diving over 2 km to hunt deep-sea squid.

children.name: Kogia
children.description: Contains 2 small, little-known species (pygmy and dwarf sperm whales) found in tropical to temperate offshore waters worldwide, notable for their shark-like false gill markings and ability to eject a cloud of reddish feces as a defence.

children.name: Delphinapterus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the beluga, an Arctic and subarctic whale uniquely lacking a dorsal fin, with a flexible melon and exceptional vocal range that earned it the nickname 'canary of the sea.'

children.name: Monodon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the narwhal, an Arctic cetacean in which males bear a spiralled ivory tusk that can reach 3 m and is now known to function as a sensory organ, living year-round in pack ice.

children.name: Berardius
children.description: Contains 2 species of giant beaked whales in the North Pacific — the largest beaked whales, reaching up to 13 m — known for deep diving and small triangular teeth at the tip of the lower jaw.

children.name: Hyperoodon
children.description: Contains 2 species of bottlenose whales (northern and southern) distinguished by their bulbous foreheads and affinity for deep cold waters, with the northern species historically hunted commercially by the Norwegians.

children.name: Indopacetus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Longman's beaked whale, one of the least-known cetaceans, found in tropical and warm temperate waters of the Indo-Pacific and recognised from relatively few confirmed sightings.

children.name: Mesoplodon
children.description: The largest beaked whale genus with 14 species distributed across all ocean basins, characterised by males bearing a single pair of large teeth erupting from the mid-lower jaw, with many species known only from strandings.

children.name: Ziphius
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Cuvier's beaked whale, the world's deepest and longest-diving mammal (exceeding 2,992 m and 137 minutes on a single breath), found in deep waters across all oceans.

children.name: Tasmacetus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Shepherd's beaked whale, unique among beaked whales in retaining a full set of functional teeth in both jaws, found in cool temperate Southern Hemisphere waters.

children.name: Delphinus
children.description: Contains 2 species of common dolphins with a distinctive hourglass pattern on their flanks, forming some of the largest dolphin schools on Earth in warm-temperate and tropical waters worldwide.

children.name: Tursiops
children.description: Contains 2 species of bottlenose dolphins — the most extensively studied cetaceans — found in coastal and oceanic waters worldwide, known for high intelligence, complex social bonds, and tool use.

children.name: Orcinus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the killer whale (orca), the largest member of the dolphin family and apex predator of all oceans, notable for its ecotypes, cooperative hunting strategies, and complex cultural traditions.

children.name: Globicephala
children.description: Contains 2 species of pilot whales — large, bulbous-headed social dolphins that travel in tight pods — found in deep offshore waters globally and infamous for mass strandings.

children.name: Grampus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Risso's dolphin, a blunt-headed, heavily scarred species found in deep temperate and tropical waters worldwide that feeds almost exclusively on squid.

children.name: Pseudorca
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the false killer whale, a large, slender, uniformly dark dolphin found in tropical and subtropical seas worldwide known for sharing prey with other dolphins and occasional fish depredation.

children.name: Orcaella
children.description: Contains 2 species of stocky, snub-nosed dolphins — the Irrawaddy dolphin of Southeast Asian rivers and coasts, and the Australian snubfin dolphin — notable for their round heads and lack of a prominent beak.

children.name: Sousa
children.description: Contains 4 species of humpback dolphins inhabiting shallow coastal and estuarine waters of the Indian and western Pacific Oceans and West Africa, named for the dorsal hump that carries a small fin in some species.

children.name: Stenella
children.description: Contains 5 species of small, slender oceanic dolphins — including spinner and spotted dolphins — widespread in tropical and warm-temperate seas and infamous for by-catch mortality in tuna purse-seine fisheries.

children.name: Steno
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the rough-toothed dolphin, a warm-water offshore species found across all tropical and subtropical oceans, distinguishable by its narrow, elongated head with a gradually sloping forehead and wrinkled teeth.

children.name: Lagenodelphis
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Fraser's dolphin, a robust, short-beaked oceanic species of tropical and subtropical waters that travels in dense, cohesive schools and was not formally described until 1956.

children.name: Lagenorhynchus
children.description: Contains 6 species of fast-swimming, short-beaked dolphins (including white-sided and dusky dolphins) ranging from cold temperate to subpolar waters of both hemispheres, often seen bow-riding and breaching.

children.name: Lissodelphis
children.description: Contains 2 species of sleek, dorsally finless dolphins — one in the North Pacific and one in the Southern Ocean — characterised by their streamlined black-and-white bodies and high-speed swimming.

children.name: Cephalorhynchus
children.description: Contains 4 species of small, stocky coastal dolphins (including Commerson's and Hector's dolphins) restricted to cold temperate waters of the Southern Hemisphere, with rounded dorsal fins and striking black-and-white patterns.

children.name: Feresa
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pygmy killer whale, a small, rarely seen tropical offshore dolphin known for its aggression toward other cetaceans despite its modest size of around 2.5 m.

children.name: Peponocephala
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the melon-headed whale, a small, gregarious tropical dolphin that congregates in schools of hundreds to thousands in deep offshore waters across the Indo-Pacific and Atlantic.

children.name: Sotalia
children.description: Contains 2 small South American dolphin species — the tucuxi of Amazonian rivers and the costero of coastal waters — among the few delphinids to inhabit freshwater, with compact builds and subtle blue-grey colouration.

children.name: Phocoena
children.description: Contains 4 porpoise species including the harbour porpoise of North Atlantic and Pacific coasts and the critically endangered vaquita of the Gulf of California, all small and shy with spade-shaped teeth.

children.name: Phocoenoides
children.description: A monotypic genus containing Dall's porpoise, the fastest small cetacean reaching up to 55 km/h, found in cold-temperate to subarctic waters of the North Pacific and identified by its bold black-and-white markings.

children.name: Neophocaena
children.description: Contains 2 species of finless porpoises inhabiting shallow coastal and riverine waters from the Persian Gulf to Japan, the only porpoises without a dorsal fin, with a ridge of tubercles along the back instead.

children.name: Platanista
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the South Asian river dolphin, found in the Ganges and Indus river systems, functionally blind with vestigial eyes and relying entirely on echolocation to navigate turbid waters.

children.name: Inia
children.description: Contains 3 species of Amazon river dolphins (botos), the largest river dolphins, found in the Amazon and Orinoco basins and recognisable by their pink colouration, flexible necks, and long beaks adapted for foraging on floodplain floors.

children.name: Pontoporia
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the La Plata dolphin (franciscana), the only river dolphin found primarily in coastal marine and estuarine waters of southeastern South America, with one of the longest beaks relative to body size of any cetacean.

children.name: Lipotes
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the baiji, a functionally extinct Yangtze river dolphin last confirmed sighted in 2002, representing the first cetacean driven to extinction by human activity.
